notes: kindness of thy youth--not so much Israel's kindness towards God, as the kindness which Israel experienced from God in their early history (compare Eze 16:8, 22, 60; 23:3, 8, 19; Ho 2:15). For Israel from the first showed perversity rather than kindness towards God (compare Ex 14:11, 12; 15:24; 32:1-7, &c.). The greater were God's favors to them from the first, the fouler was their ingratitude in forsaking Him (Jer 2:3, 5, &c.). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

espousals--the intervals between Israel's betrothal to God at the exodus from Egypt, and the formal execution of the marriage contract at Sinai. EWALD takes the "kindness" and "love" to be Israel's towards God at first (Ex 19:8; 24:3; 35:20-29; 36:5; Jos 24:16-17). But compare De 32:16, 17; Eze 16:5, 6, 15, 22 ("days of thy youth") implies that the love here meant was on God's side, not Israel's. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

thou wentest after me in . . . wilderness--the next act of God's love, His leading them in the desert without needing any strange god, such as they since worshipped, to help Him (De 2:7; 32:12). Jer 2:6 shows it is God's "leading" of them, not their following after God in the wilderness, which is implied. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

first-fruits of his increase--that is, of Jehovah's produce. As the first-fruits of the whole produce of the land were devoted to God (Ex 23:19; Nu 18:12, 13), so Israel was devoted to Him as the first-fruit and representative nation among all nations. So the spiritual Israel (Jas 1:18; Re 14:4). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

devour--carrying on the image of first-fruits which were eaten before the Lord by the priests as the Lord's representatives; all who ate (injured) Jehovah's first-fruits (Israel), contracted guilt: for example, Amalek, the Amorites, &c., were extirpated for their guilt towards Israel. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
holiness unto the Lord--that is, was consecrated to the service of Jehovah (Ex 19:5, 6). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

The "fathers" mentioned in this prophecy may have been those of the long, idolatrous reign of Hezekiah's son Manasseh and grandson Amon (ca. 697-640 B.C.; 2 Kings 21; Jeremiah 15:4). Many of the evils Jeremiah condemned and fought against throughout his mission were the religious and social sins made common in that fifty-seven year period. The reforms of King Josiah no doubt had some good effects; but just as good character grows like a tree and evil multiplies like weeds, so did Judah slip back into evil ways after Josiah's death. (Ellis Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, 543.)
walked after vanity--contrasted with "walkest after me in the wilderness" (Jer 2:2): then I was their guide in the barren desert; now they take idols as their guides. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R.

deserts . . . pits--The desert between Mount Sinai and Palestine abounds in chasms and pits, in which beasts of burden often sink down to the knees. "Shadow of death" refers to the darkness of the caverns amidst the rocky precipices (De 8:15; 32:10). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Chittim--Cyprus, colonized by Phœnicians, who built in it the city of Citium, the modern Chitti. Then the term came to be applied to all maritime coasts of the Mediterranean, especially Greece (Nu 24:24; Isa 23:1; Da 11:30). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

In the book of Revelation, Alpha and Omega (Christ) promises to give freely to him who is athirst "the fountain of the water of life" (see Revelation 21:6; see also D&C 10:66). The book of Isaiah refers to the day of restoration when "with joy shall ye draw water out of the wells of salvation" (Isaiah 12:3; see also 55:1). Latter-day Church leaders often use the phrase "broken cisterns that can hold no water" in describing various fruitless practices or concepts. Elder Marion D. Hanks made this application of the expression: The substitutions we fashion to take the place of God in our lives truly hold no water. To the measure we thus refuse the "living water," we miss the joy we could have. (Conference Report, Apr. 1972, p. 127.) (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah, p.24)

11-13. Israel's forsaking of the Lord and her following after false gods of other nations are enumerated as separate evils. That Christ is the "fountain of living waters" spoken of and thus the God of Jeremiah's people is clearly shown in this scripture. Christ told the woman of Samaria that he would give her living water that would spring up unto everlasting life (see John 4:10-14; see also D&C 63:23).

kingdom; it is the doctrines of the gospel" (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Doctrinal New Testament Commentary, 1:151; see also John 4:6-14; 7:37; Isa. 55:1). Jesus is the "fountain of living waters" (Jer. 2:13; 17:13; compare Isa. 33:21), or the divine source of all truth. (See also Fountain.) (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie and Donald W. Parry, A Guide to Scriptural Symbols, p.107) Christ has provided us the gift of a prophet. Of all mortal men, we should keep our eyes most firmly fixed on the captain-the prophet, seer, and revelator, and President of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. This is the man who stands closest to the fountain of living waters (Jeremiah 2:13; 1 Nephi 11:25). There are some heavenly instructions for us that we can only receive through the prophet. A good way to measure your standing with the Lord is to see how you feel about, and act upon, the inspired words of His earthly representative, the prophet-president. The inspired words of the President are not to be trifled with. All men are entitled to inspiration, and various men are entitled to revelation for their particular assignment. But only one man stands as the Lord's spokesman to the Church and the world, and he is the President of the Church. The words of all other men should be weighed against his inspired words. Though His prophet is mortal, God will not let him lead His Church astray. (See Discourses of Wilford Woodruff, pp. 212-13.) (Ezra Taft Benson, The Teachings of Ezra Taft Benson, p.140)

How natural it was, then, for the Lord and his prophets to use the search for water as a pattern for the search for salvation. Just as a man is spared temporally by drinking the life-giving liquids of mortality, so he is saved eternally by downing great draughts of living water. Moses at Meribah smote the rock and a great flood of water gushed forth to give drink to all Israel and their cattle, thus teaching them that if they would look to Jehovah, by whose power the miracle was wrought, they might drink forever from streams of living water. (Num. 20:1-13.) Jeremiah proclaimed that "the Lord" was "the fountain of living waters." (Jer. 17:13.) And the Lord himself, complaining about the rebellious nature of his people, said: "For my people have committed two evils; they have forsaken me the fountain of living waters, and hewed them out cisterns, broken cisterns, that can hold no water." (Jer. 2:13.) After his resurrection this same Jehovah, identifying himself as the Lord Jesus Christ, spoke similarly to Moroni in these words: "Faith, hope and charity bringeth unto me-the fountain of all righteousness." (Ether 12:28.) (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, The Promised Messiah: The First Coming of Christ, p.204)

In verse 13 the two evils committed by Judah are told in figurative terms: (1) They have forsaken the fountain (Jehovah) of living water (life), and (2) they have hewn out broken cisterns (gods) which can hold no water (life). Then the image is changed, and the Lord states that Israel had partaken of the waters of "Sihor" (the Nile) and of "the river" (v. 18) (the Euphrates). In other words, they drank the spiritual waters of Egypt and Babylon and were filled with the lifeless water of idolatry. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 236)

Too many of our Heavenly Father's children spend their precious lives carving out broken cisterns of worldly gain that cannot hold the living water that satisfies fully their natural thirst for everlasting truth. (Joseph B. Wirthlin, in Conference Report, Apr. 1995, 23)

quotes.text: In other words, they drank the spiritual waters of Egypt and Babylon and were filled with the lifeless water of idolatry. (Old Testament Student Manual: 1 Kings-Malachi, p. 236) Too many of our Heavenly Father's children spend their precious lives carving out broken cisterns of worldly gain that cannot hold the living water that satisfies fully their natural thirst for everlasting truth.
quotes.attr: Joseph B. Wirthlin, in Conference Report, Apr. 1995, 23

quotes.text: It is life-giving "water" of new birth unto salvation and atonement. (Ellis T. Rasmussen, A Latter-day Saint Commentary on the Old Testament, p.513) Too many of our Heavenly Father's children spend their precious lives carving out broken cisterns of worldly gain that cannot hold the living water that satisfies fully their natural thirst for everlasting truth.
quotes.attr: Joseph B. Wirthlin, in Conference Report, Apr. 1995, 23

quotes.text: But only one man stands as the Lord's spokesman to the Church and the world, and he is the President of the Church. The words of all other men should be weighed against his inspired words. Though His prophet is mortal, God will not let him lead His Church astray.
quotes.attr: See Discourses of Wilford Woodruff, pp. 212-13.

quotes.text: Though His prophet is mortal, God will not let him lead His Church astray. (See Discourses of Wilford Woodruff, pp. 212-13.)
quotes.attr: Ezra Taft Benson, The Teachings of Ezra Taft Benson, p.140

notes: is he a homeborn slave--No. "Israel is Jehovah's son, even His first-born" (Ex 4:22). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Noph . . . Tahapanes--Memphis, capital of Lower Egypt, on the west bank of the Nile, near the pyramids of Gizeh, opposite the site of modern Cairo. Daphne, on the Tanitic branch of the Nile, near Pelusium, on the frontier of Egypt towards Palestine. Isa 30:4 contracts it, Hanes. These two cities, one the capital, the other that with which the Jews came most in contact, stand for the whole of Egypt. Tahapanes takes its name from a goddess, Tphnet [CHAMPOLLION]. Memphis is from Man-nofri, "the abode of good men"; written in Hebrew, Moph (Ho 9:6), or Noph. The reference is to the coming invasion of Judah by Pharaoh-necho of Egypt, on his return from the Euphrates, when he deposed Jehoahaz and levied a heavy tribute on the land (2Ki 23:33-35). Josiah's death in battle with the same Pharaoh is probably included (2Ki 23:29, 30). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Sihor--that is, the black river, in Greek, Melas ("black"), the Nile: so called from the black deposit or soil it leaves after the inundation (Isa 23:3). The Septuagint identifies it with Gihon, one of the rivers of Paradise. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Indeed, moral standards must be maintained. In large measure, those who are disobedient punish themselves. As the Lord said through Jeremiah: "Thine own wickedness shall correct thee, and thy backslidings shall reprove thee." Those entrusted with judicial responsibility in the kingdom of God must see that the Church remains clean so that the living waters of life flow unimpeded. (James E. Faust, in Conference Report, Oct. 1997, 77)

This ancient prophet points out plainly that in disobedience the children of men always find a penalty involved, just as much so as there is a reward for rendering obedience to the Lord. (Joseph L. Wirthlin, Conference Report, Apr. 1941, pp. 62-63.)

quotes.text: Indeed, moral standards must be maintained. In large measure, those who are disobedient punish themselves. As the Lord said through Jeremiah: "Thine own wickedness shall correct thee, and thy backslidings shall reprove thee." notes: 20-30. The second evil spoken of in verse 13, that of following after the false gods of other nations, is further described in these verses. Jehovah reminds Israel of times past when he delivered her out of bondage and she then covenanted to keep his commandments; yet on many subsequent occasions, Israel wandered astray and played the harlot by having an affair with Baal or Baalim, the false gods of the surrounding nations. Baal was worshiped in high places among groves of trees-and this included temple prostitution. Therefore, the description of Israel as a harlot carries a double meaning: a literal act of immorality and a figurative representation of Israel's forsaking her husband (Jehovah) to have an affair with another god. The Lord next declares that Israel had been planted by him as a noble vine, wholly a good seed, and asks why she has become a degenerate and strange vine to him. He further describes her apostasy as an internal or spiritual one. and her uncleanliness as that which cannot be washed away with soap or cleansing agents. Israel's or Judah's pretended impotence is refuted by the Lord. and he compares his wayward people to two familiar animals. The first is a swift dromedary which has gone against her training. A dromedary is a one-humped camel especially bred and trained for riding. Israel is therefore accused of knowingly forsaking the commandments of God. Secondly, the Lord compares her to a wild ass who has learned the way of the wilderness and is free to follow the desires of her heart. He then asks, "In her occasion who can turn her away?" The question seems to be, "Who can take away her freedom?" The King James Version of the Bible marginal note reads "or, reverse it?" The Joseph Smith Translation of the Bible (often referred to as the "Inspired Version") renders the answer, "All they that seek her will weary themselves; in her month they shall not find her" (verse 24; italics added). If she follows her natural instincts for freedom, she will not be caught. Other commentators, following the corrupted text, suggest that "on her occasion" and "in her month" refer to the male animals seeking her out in the time of her periodic breeding seasons, during which time she will be caught. The Joseph Smith Translation of the Bible (often referred to as the "Inspired Version") fits the context of the following verses better. The Lord admonishes the wild ass (Judah) to "withhold thy foot from being unshod, and

thy throat from thirst." In other words, she is invited to maintain her freedom by avoiding those who are seeking to capture her as she satisfies her thirst at the waterhole. However, Israel, or Judah, does not choose to maintain her freedom; rather she has loved strangers (other gods) in the past and now chooses to go after them again. She succumbs to the will of the flesh, the natural man or ways of the world. rather than follow the enticings of the Spirit (see Mosiah 3:19). The Lord declares that as a thief who is caught is ashamed, so will Judah be ashamed for worshiping gods of wood and stone who cannot save her in times of trouble. He further predicts that she will plead with him for deliverance. The Lord concludes with a lament over his attempts in the past to correct Israel, but she has always reiected his prophets and killed them with their own swords. (Monte S. Nyman, The Words of Jeremiah , p.24-25)

Israel Symbolized by Vineyard and Vines.-The aptness of our Lord's representation of Israel as a vineyard could not have escaped the perception of the Jews, to whom Old Testament similes of analogous form were familiar figures. Notable among others is the striking picture presented by Isaiah (5:1-7), in which the well provided vineyard is shown as producing wild grapes only, for which grievous disappointment of his expectations the owner determined to break down the wall, remove the hedge, and leave the vineyard to its fate of abandonment. The explication of the parable voiced by Isaiah is thus given: "For the vineyard of the Lord of hosts is the house of Israel, and the men of Judah his pleasant plant: and he looked for judgment, but behold oppression; for righteousness, but behold a cry." The worthlessness of a vine save only for its fruit was set forth by the Lord through His prophet Ezekiel (15:2-5); and truly it is so, that the wood of the grape plant is fit for nothing but burning; the whole vine as wood is inferior to a branch from a forest tree (verse 3). And Israel is represented as such a vine, precious if but fruitful, otherwise nothing but fuel and that of poor quality. The psalmist sang of the vine that Jehovah had brought out of Egypt and which, planted with care and hedged about, had flourished even with goodly boughs; but the favor of the Lord had been turned from the vine, and it had been left desolate (Ps. 80:8-16). For further allusions see Isa. 27:2-6; Jer. 2:21; Ezek. 19:10-14; Hosea 10:1. (James E. Talmage, Jesus the Christ: A Study of the Messiah and His Mission According to Holy Scriptures Both Ancient and Modern, p.502)
nitre - carbonate of soda from the root nathar "to start up, tremble, shake, spring up"
dromedary--rather, a "young she-camel." (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

occasion--either from a Hebrew root, "to meet"; "her meeting (with the male for sexual intercourse), who can avert it?" Or better from an Arabic root: "her heat (sexual impulse), who can allay it?" [MAURER]. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
Withhold, &c.--that is, abstain from incontinence; figuratively for idolatry [HOUBIGANT]. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
stone (eben) [both of these reference symbols of God, but they have turned them]
The Hebrew collocation is, "O, the generation, ye," that is, "O ye who now live." idolatry [HOUBIGANT]. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
Oriental women greatly pride themselves on their ornaments (compare Isa 61:10). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
attire--girdles for the breast. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Why trimmest--MAURER translates, "How skilfully thou dost prepare thy way," &c. But see 2Ki 9:30. "Trimmest" best suits the image of one decking herself as a harlot. (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)

Also--not only art thou polluted with idolatry, but also with the guilt of shedding innocent blood [MAURER]. ROSENMULLER not so well translates, "even in thy skirts," &c.; that is, there is no part of thee (not even thy skirts) that is not stained with innocent blood (Jer 19:4; 2Ki 21:16; Ps 106:38). See as to innocent blood shed, not as here in honor of idols, but of prophets for having reproved them (Jer 2:30; Jer 26:20-23). (Jeremiah Commentary by A. R. FAUSSET)
